dialogue.utterance: Unit 6 has breathing problem in [CITY] at --Address Redacted--
Hi, I'm Lucas. You called for help because of breathing issues, right?
Yeah, I'm having a really hard time breathing. Started about 20 minutes ago.
Great. Can you tell me your name and where we are right now, sir?
John Haberman, this is my kitchen in [CITY], [STATE].
What is the date today, sir?
March 11th, 2019. I keep track, but it's hard to forget a day like this.
Your airway is clear and maintainable. I'm going to assess your breathing now. Is that okay?
Mr. Haberman, I'm going to look at your breathing now. Let's check for rate and signs of labored breathing.
I'm going to take your blood pressure now. Can you keep your hand relaxed, please, sir?
Your skin looks warm and dry.
Your neck is supple with no pain or noticeable vein distension. That's good.
Yeah. I've been on oxygen since I went to bed, but it's not helping much.
Your pulse is 109, which is a bit fast. I'll take the rest one at a time.
Your breathing is at 23 breaths per minute. It's fast and you're using extra muscles to breathe.
Your blood pressure is very high at 197 over 171. I'm going to bring the stretcher around to the back to prepare for transport.
We are going to check your blood sugar now. It's 196. We'll also apply the basic EKG sensors now. Okay with you?
I'm going to administer breathing treatments using a nebulizer. I'll be giving you Albuterol and Ipratropium to help with your breathing. Okay?
Your oxygen saturation is at 94% on room air. I'm going to start the nebulizer treatments and place you on a portable oxygen at high flow rate. This should help you breathe easier.
I heard diminished lung sounds on your left and some wheezing and rhonchi in your right upper lobes. You're using accessory muscles now, which is common with respiratory distress.
Your mouth is moist with pink mucous membranes and no signs of ulceration.
I'm going to apply a 4-lead EKG now to monitor your heart. This is non-invasive and should not cause you any discomfort.
I'm going to place you on Continuous Positive Airway Pressure (CPAP) with the nebulizer line connected. This should assist your breathing and improve expansion.
I'm going to start an IV in your left wrist so we can administer medications more quickly.
I will now administer 10mg of Dexamethasone intravenously to help reduce airway inflammation. Okay with you?
I'm securing the IV line now and delivering the medication. You may feel a slight cold sensation at the site as it goes in.
That was per our standard protocol for respiratory distress. Treatment is ongoing and effective.
Your breathing has improved. It's now at 17 breaths a minute, and your heart rate has decreased to 84. You're also now sitting up in a more relaxed position.
I'm going to run a second nebulizer treatment with Albuterol. We expect another improvement. It's a single dose this time to help further open your airways. Would that be fine with you?

I feel much better. I can talk in full sentences now. I have asthma and COPD, and I had bronchitis last week. I had pneumonia in the past, but this seems different. It came on fast. I've been on home O2 and it helped a little, but not much. I really appreciated the first treatment.

I noticed you sat up in a tripod position earlier to help you breathe. You're now alert and oriented in all four respects-person, place, time, and event. Your extremities have good movement and strength with no signs of edema.
How is your breathing now, sir? You seem to be speaking comfortably now.
You have not reported any chest pain at any point during our treatment.

The EKG detected atrial fibrillation, premature atrial contractions, premature ventricular contractions, sinus arrhythmia, and sinus tachycardia. I'm going to run the 12-lead EKG now as well to give the hospital more detailed information.
I'm going to secure you to the stretcher now for your transport to [COMPANY_NAME]. They're ready for us there. Please remain relaxed. You'll be more comfortable as you lie back. Anything you need?
I'm good. Let's go. Just make it a smooth ride.
Your cough has been non-productive throughout, with no mucus or phlegm produced at any point during our assessment.
Your pulse is now at 84-within the normal range for someone in your condition.
You're alert and oriented. Your AVPU is normal. No change in consciousness or confusion noted.
The 12-lead EKG interpretation is showing atrial fibrillation and some premature contractions, but no troponin elevations or ischemic changes thus far.
You did not report any pain, and neither my partner nor I have documented any throughout the entire assessment and treatment.
Ready when you are. [COMPANY_NAME] is our destination. Transport is authorized.
Initial vitals were recorded and include: pulse 109, respirations 23, BP 197 over 171, glucose 196, SpO2 94. EKG showed findings indicating atrial fibrillation and some arrhythmias.
Your respiratory rate looked high at first, but it's now effectively reduced to 17 after the treatments.
Your oxygen saturation improved to 99%, which is good. Your breathing is much better now.
I noticed retractions and the use of accessory muscles earlier. This is a sign of increased work of breathing, but they've reduced now with treatment.
Your SpO2 was 94% initially, which is a mild sign of hypoxia. It's improved now to 99% with the CPAP and oxygen therapy.
Throughout the entire evaluation and treatment you have not experienced or reported any pain. Pain level is at 0.
You're now fully secured on the stretcher with safety straps in place. You'll be as safe and as comfortable as possible the entire ride.
